item.content:encoded:
⏱️ Prep Time: 30 minutes

🍳 Cook Time: 0 minutes

⏰ Total Time: N/A minutes

🍽️ Servings: 6

📊 Difficulty: Medium

🥘 Ingredients

  • 1 large Napa cabbage (about 2 pounds), quartered and rinsed
  • 1/4 cup sea salt
  • 4 cups water
  • 1 tablespoon grated ginger
  • 4 cloves garlic, minced
  • 1 tablespoon sugar
  • 3 tablespoons Korean red pepper flakes (gochugaru), adjust to taste
  • 4 green onions, chopped
  • 1 medium carrot, julienned
  • 2 tablespoons fish sauce

👩‍🍳 Instructions

  1. Start by cutting the Napa cabbage into quarters and rinsing it well to remove any dirt. Then, sprinkle the sea salt evenly between the layers of the cabbage leaves. This step is crucial because the salt draws out moisture from the cabbage, which softens it and creates the brine needed for fermentation. Let the salted cabbage sit submerged in water for about 2 hours, flipping it every 30 minutes to ensure even salting. Avoid skipping this step or rushing it; under-salting can lead to soggy kimchi that doesn’t ferment properly.
  2. While the cabbage is soaking, prepare the kimchi paste. In a bowl, mix grated ginger, minced garlic, sugar, Korean red pepper flakes, and fish sauce. This paste is the flavor powerhouse of your kimchi, combining spicy, sweet, and umami notes. Adjust the amount of chili flakes if you prefer milder or hotter kimchi. Mixing these ingredients thoroughly helps the flavors meld and ensures an even distribution when you coat the cabbage later.
  3. After the cabbage has soaked, rinse it thoroughly under cold water to remove excess salt. This step prevents your kimchi from becoming overly salty. Drain the cabbage well and gently squeeze out any remaining water. If the cabbage is too wet, the fermentation might be slowed down or uneven. Getting the right moisture balance is key to a crisp yet tangy kimchi.
  4. In a large mixing bowl, combine the drained cabbage with the prepared kimchi paste, chopped green onions, and julienned carrot. Use your hands (wear gloves if you want to avoid chili burns) to massage the paste evenly into every leaf of the cabbage. This ensures every bite is flavorful and the fermentation is consistent throughout. Don’t be shy about working the paste in well—it’s what makes homemade kimchi taste so good compared to store-bought.
  5. Pack the kimchi mixture tightly into a clean glass jar or fermentation container, pressing down firmly to remove air pockets. Leaving air inside can cause uneven fermentation or mold growth. Seal the jar loosely to allow gases to escape during fermentation. Store the jar at room temperature, ideally around 65-70°F, for 1 to 3 days to start fermenting. You’ll notice bubbles forming and a tangy smell developing, which means the good bacteria are doing their work.
  6. After initial fermentation, move the kimchi to the refrigerator to slow down the process. The flavors will continue to develop and mellow over time. It’s best after about a week but can be enjoyed anytime. Refrigeration also keeps the kimchi crunchy and fresh. Remember, the longer it sits, the tangier it gets, so taste periodically to find your preferred level.
  7. When serving, use clean utensils to scoop out kimchi to avoid introducing contaminants. It pairs wonderfully as a side with rice, grilled meats, or even scrambled eggs. You can also chop it up and add it to soups or stews for a flavor boost. The versatility of kimchi makes it a staple in my kitchen for adding depth and spice to everyday meals.
  8. If you want to make a milder version, reduce the Korean red pepper flakes and increase the sugar slightly to balance the heat. For a vegan option, omit fish sauce and substitute with soy sauce or tamari. These small tweaks allow you to customize the kimchi to your family’s tastes without losing the essence of the dish.
  9. Keep an eye on your fermenting kimchi for any signs of mold or off smells. It’s normal for some white bubbles or foam to form, but if you see fuzzy mold or a rotten odor, discard it. Cleanliness during preparation and proper salting help prevent spoilage. Trust your senses—good kimchi smells fresh, spicy, and tangy, never rotten.
